title: Mural crown

infobox.value: ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *corona muralis*) is a [crown](/wiki/Crown_(headgear) "Crown (headgear)") or headpiece representing [city walls](/wiki/City_wall "City wall"), [towers](/wiki/Fortified_tower "Fortified tower"), or [fortresses](/wiki/Fortress "Fortress"). In [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), it was an emblem of [tutelary deities](/wiki/Tutelary_deities "Tutelary deities") who watched over a city, and among the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") a [military decoration](/wiki/Military_decoration "Military decoration"). Later the mural crown developed into a symbol of European [heraldry](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry"), mostly for cities and towns, and in the 19th and 20th centuries was used in some republican heraldry.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Heraldic crown resembling a wall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Tetradrachm_Smyrna_160-150_obverse_CdM_Paris.jpg/250px-Tetradrachm_Smyrna_160-150_obverse_CdM_Paris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tetradrachm_Smyrna_160-150_obverse_CdM_Paris.jpg)

Mural crown on city goddess (on a silver *[tetradrachm](/wiki/Tetradrachm "Tetradrachm")* coin issued by [Smyrna](/wiki/Smyrna "Smyrna"), 160–150 BC)

A **mural crown** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *corona muralis*) is a [crown](/wiki/Crown_(headgear) "Crown (headgear)") or headpiece representing [city walls](/wiki/City_wall "City wall"), [towers](/wiki/Fortified_tower "Fortified tower"), or [fortresses](/wiki/Fortress "Fortress"). In [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), it was an emblem of [tutelary deities](/wiki/Tutelary_deities "Tutelary deities") who watched over a city, and among the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") a [military decoration](/wiki/Military_decoration "Military decoration"). Later the mural crown developed into a symbol of European [heraldry](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry"), mostly for cities and towns, and in the 19th and 20th centuries was used in some republican heraldry.

## Usage in ancient times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mural_crown&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Usage in ancient times")]

Early appearances of the mural crown occur in the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), where they resemble crenelations on Mesopotamian and Persian buildings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Darius_the_great._jpg.jpg/250px-Darius_the_great._jpg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darius_the_great._jpg.jpg)

Darius the Great wearing an early mural crown on the [Behistun inscription](/wiki/Behistun_Inscription "Behistun Inscription")

In [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") culture, a mural crown identified [tutelary deities](/wiki/Tutelary_deity "Tutelary deity") such as the goddess [Tyche](/wiki/Tyche "Tyche"), the embodiment of the fortunes of a city. The high cylindrical *[polos](/wiki/Polos "Polos")* of [Rhea](/wiki/Rhea_(mythology) "Rhea (mythology)") was rendered as a mural crown for [Cybele](/wiki/Cybele "Cybele") in Hellenistic times, as patron of a city.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Graeco-Roman goddess [Roma](/wiki/Roma_(mythology) "Roma (mythology)")'s attributes on [Greek coinage](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_coinage "Ancient Greek coinage") usually include her mural crown, signifying Rome's status as a loyal protector of [Hellenic city-states](/wiki/Hellenic_city-state "Hellenic city-state").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The mural crown became an [ancient Roman military decoration](/wiki/Roman_military_decorations_and_punishments "Roman military decorations and punishments"). The *corona muralis* (Latin for "walled crown") was a golden crown, or a circle of gold intended to resemble a [battlement](/wiki/Battlement "Battlement"), bestowed upon the soldier who first climbed the wall of a besieged city or fortress to successfully place the standard (flag) of the attacking army upon it.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Maxfield1981-4) The Roman mural crown was made of gold, and decorated with turrets,[[5]](#cite_note-5) as is the heraldic version. As it was among the highest order of military decorations, it was not awarded to a claimant until after a strict investigation.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The *[rostrata](/wiki/Rostrata "Rostrata")* mural crown, composed of the *[rostra](/wiki/Rostrum_(ship) "Rostrum (ship)")* indicative of captured ships, was assigned as naval prize to the first in a boarding party, similar to the [naval crown](/wiki/Naval_crown "Naval crown").

## Heraldic use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mural_crown&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Heraldic use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Corona_muralis.svg/250px-Corona_muralis.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Corona_muralis.svg)

A heraldic mural crown

The Roman military decoration was subsequently employed in European [heraldry](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry"), where the term denoted a [crown](/wiki/Crown_(headgear) "Crown (headgear)") modeled after the walls of a castle, which may be tinctured *[or](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)")* (gold), *[argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent")* (silver), *[gules](/wiki/Gules "Gules")* (red), or *proper* (i.e. stone-coloured). In 19th-century Germany, mural crowns (**Mauerkronen**) came to be adopted for the arms of cities, with increasingly specific details: "Residential (i.e. having a [*royal* residence](/wiki/Residenz "Residenz")) cities and capital towns usually bear a **Mauerkrone** with five towers, large towns one with four towers, smaller towns one with three", observed [Arthur Charles Fox-Davies](/wiki/Arthur_Charles_Fox-Davies "Arthur Charles Fox-Davies"), in *A Complete Guide to Heraldry*, adding "Strict regulations in the matter do not yet exist" and warning that the usage was not British.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Mural crowns were used, rather than royal crowns, for [medieval](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") and modern Italian *[comuni](/wiki/Comune "Comune")*. A mural-crowned lady, [Italia Turrita](/wiki/Italia_Turrita "Italia Turrita"), personifies Italy. In Italy, *comuni* and some provinces and military corps have mural crowns on their coats of arms: gold with five towers for cities, and silver with nine-towered for others. The coat of arms of the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") had a mural crown.

In the early 20th century [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") established strict rules for its municipal heraldry, in which each coat of arms contains a mural crown, with three silver towers signifying a village or an urban parish, four silver towers representing a town, five silver towers standing for a city and five gold towers for a capital city. The Portuguese rules are also applied to most municipal coats of arms of Brazil and some other members of the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries").

Romanian municipal coats of arms contain a mural crown, with one or three towers for villages and communes, five and seven towers for towns and municipalities.

The eagle on the [coat of arms of Austria](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Austria "Coat of arms of Austria") wears a mural crown to signify its status as a republic. This is in contrast to the royal crowns that adorned the [double-headed eagle](/wiki/Double-headed_eagle "Double-headed eagle") (and the imperial crown positioned above it) in the [coat of arms of Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Austria-Hungary "Coat of arms of Austria-Hungary") until their defeat in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The mural-crowned eagle was abandoned under the [clerico-fascist](/wiki/Clerical_fascism "Clerical fascism") [Federal State of Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria") from 1934, but was reinstated in [Allied-occupied Austria](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Austria "Allied-occupied Austria") following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and remains in place to this day.

* [![The coat of arms of Krasnoyarsk is topped with a form of mural crown, which is the golden five-tower coronet of rank of a Russian federal subject administrative centre.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Coat_of_Arms_of_Krasnoyarsk_%28Krasnoyarsk_krai%29.svg/120px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Krasnoyarsk_%28Krasnoyarsk_krai%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Krasnoyarsk_(Krasnoyarsk_krai).svg "The coat of arms of Krasnoyarsk is topped with a form of mural crown, which is the golden five-tower coronet of rank of a Russian federal subject administrative centre.")

  The [coat of arms of Krasnoyarsk](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Krasnoyarsk "Coat of arms of Krasnoyarsk") is topped with a form of mural crown, which is the golden five-tower [coronet of rank](/wiki/Crown_(heraldry)#As_a_display_of_rank "Crown (heraldry)") of a Russian federal subject administrative centre.
* [![Coat of Arms of Kryvyi Rih, Ukraine](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Coat_of_Arms_of_Kryvyi_Rih.svg/120px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Kryvyi_Rih.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Kryvyi_Rih.svg "Coat of Arms of Kryvyi Rih, Ukraine")

  [Coat of Arms of Kryvyi Rih](/wiki/Coat_of_Arms_of_Kryvyi_Rih "Coat of Arms of Kryvyi Rih"), Ukraine
* [![Coat of arms of the City of Zrenjanin, Serbia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Coat_of_Arms_of_Zrenjanin.png/120px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Zrenjanin.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Zrenjanin.png "Coat of arms of the City of Zrenjanin, Serbia")

  Coat of arms of the City of [Zrenjanin](/wiki/Zrenjanin "Zrenjanin"), Serbia
* [![Coat of arms of Ninove, Belgium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Ninove_wapen1.svg/120px-Ninove_wapen1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ninove_wapen1.svg "Coat of arms of Ninove, Belgium")

  Coat of arms of [Ninove](/wiki/Ninove "Ninove"), Belgium

[*Étude sur la déesse grecque Tyché*](https://archive.org/details/tudesurlades00alluoft) (in French). Paris. pp. [187](https://archive.org/details/tudesurlades00alluoft/page/187)–92.`{{cite book}}`: CS1 maint: location missing publisher ([link](/wiki/Category:CS1_maint:_location_missing_publisher "Category:CS1 maint: location missing publisher"))
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Mellor, R., "The Goddess Roma" in Haase, W., Temporini, H., (eds), *Aufstieg und Niedergang der romischen Welt*, de Gruyter, 1991, pp 60–63.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** [Aulus Gellius](/wiki/Aulus_Gellius "Aulus Gellius"), *Noctes Attici*, V.6.4; [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy"), *Ab Urbe Condita*, XXVI.48
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Maxfield1981_4-0)** [Valerie A. Maxfield](/wiki/Valerie_Maxfield "Valerie Maxfield") (1 January 1981). [*The Military Decorations of the Roman Army*](https://books.google.com/books?id=Nuex2PW7QR0C&pg=PA77). University of California Press. pp. 77–. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-520-04499-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-520-04499-9 "Special:BookSources/978-0-520-04499-9"). [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20230403083633/https://books.google.com/books?id=Nuex2PW7QR0C&pg=PA77) from the original on 3 April 2023. Retrieved 23 October 2016.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** *muri pinnis* according to Aulus Gellius
6. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Livy. l.c.; cf. [Suetonius](/wiki/Suetonius "Suetonius"), *[Lives of the Twelve Caesars](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Twelve_Caesars "Lives of the Twelve Caesars")*, [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") 25.
